cards.back: A synchronization algorithm that uses two variables, one read and one write, to ensure that a shared resource is accessed by multiple processes in a mutually exclusive manner.

A process P first reads the value of the shared variable V, then writes its own value to V. Meanwhile, another process Q does the same. If both P and Q read the same value, they both write their values to V, resulting in a deadlock. To avoid this, Peterson's solution ensures that if P and Q both read the same value, P waits until Q has written its value before writing its own value.

cards.front: What are the two variables used in Peterson's solution?
cards.back: The two variables are V (the shared variable) and Q (the variable indicating the current process).

cards.front: What is the time complexity of Peterson's solution?
cards.back: The time complexity of Peterson's solution is O(1), as it involves a constant number of operations regardless of the number of processes.

cards.front: Is Peterson's solution a deadlock-free algorithm?
cards.back: No, Peterson's solution can lead to a deadlock if two or more processes read the same value and then write their values, without one of them releasing the resource.
